**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"** (*Psychological Bulletin*)
A meta-analysis of 128 experiments showing that tangible extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks people find inherently interesting. Often cited as foundational support for prioritizing intrinsic interest over external incentives.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets"** (*PNAS*)
Tracking over 10,000 cadets, internal motives (genuine interest in the work) predicted better long-term career outcomes, while instrumental motives (pay, status) weakened those benefits even when held alongside internal ones. The closest real support for your friends' claim.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** (*Psychological Bulletin*)
Intrinsic motivation predicts the quality of performance, while extrinsic incentives better predict quantity, and the two operate jointly rather than in opposition. Directly contradicts the "focus almost exclusively on interest" framing — both matter.

**Judge et al. (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** (*Journal of Vocational Behavior*)
Across 86 studies, the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (~0.15). Suggests a modest salary bump alone is a poor reason to switch jobs.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** (*Psychological Science*)
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests are developed sustain engagement across domains. Implies "less interesting" work (like the OpenAI role) can become interesting, especially if it fulfills other goals like leading a team.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007), "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features"** (*Journal of Applied Psychology*)
A meta-analysis of 259 studies finding that autonomy, task variety, significance, and social characteristics each independently predict satisfaction and performance. Job satisfaction is multidimensional — task interest is one input among several, and a leadership role changes autonomy and significance substantially.

**Nikolova & Cnossen (2020), "What Makes Work Meaningful and Why Economists Should Care About It"** (*Labour Economics*)
Using European survey data, autonomy, competence, and relatedness explain far more variance in meaningful work than income or benefits do. Note that team leadership can directly serve competence and relatedness needs.

---
One-sentence verdict: the research supports "interest matters a lot and money less than people think," but not "focus on interest almost exclusively" — and it suggests your long-held desire to lead a team is exactly the kind of internally-motivated goal the literature says to take seriously.

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" (Harvard Business Review)**

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**

Across 128 experiments, tangible extrinsic rewards tended to undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Suggests that taking a job primarily for money can erode the enjoyment you'd otherwise get from the work.

People who view their work as a "calling" (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations. The effect held even within the same occupation.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analyzing ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers, the strongest predictor of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work. Notably, even managers vastly underestimated this factor relative to incentives.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved" (PNAS)**
This adversarial collaboration found that happiness does continue rising with income well beyond $75k for most people, overturning the famous earlier plateau finding. Money isn't nothing — though the marginal effect of a modest raise at already-high salaries is small.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interestingness per se. Relevant because leading a team (something you've always wanted) could satisfy autonomy and competence needs even if the topic is duller.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)**
Job satisfaction is predicted by five characteristics: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — interesting content is only part of the picture. A leadership role can score high on autonomy and significance even with less interesting subject matter.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting salient features and underweighting adaptation. Cuts both ways here: you may adapt to the boredom of less interesting work — or the thrill of leading a team may fade faster than you expect.

Using data on sales workers, the best individual contributors who were promoted into management often performed worse as managers, since IC skill doesn't predict management skill. Worth weighing whether you want to lead a team because you'll be good at and enjoy it, or because it's a status marker.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right that interesting work and intrinsic motivation are underrated relative to pay — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy, growth, and fit with long-held goals (like leading a team) are comparably strong predictors of satisfaction.

Well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interestingness alone. Important nuance against your friends' "almost exclusively" framing: leading a team could satisfy autonomy and growth needs that pure research work does not.

Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Suggests asking which role offers more frequent, tangible progress — which isn't automatically the more "interesting" one.

People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests can be developed sustain engagement in new domains. My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning:
I need to find relevant research papers on whether interesting work is underrated and should be prioritized. I'm thinking about key studies on intrinsic motivation, job satisfaction, and how people actually value different aspects of their work—papers like Amabile's work on intrinsic motivation and creativity, and Wrzesniewski's research on job crafting and meaning.

I'm also considering meta-analyses that examine the relationship between pay and satisfaction, studies on affective forecasting that show people often mispredicttheir own happiness, and research suggesting that interests can be developed rather than just discovered—which challenges the idea that you should only pursue work you already find interesting. Wrzesniewski's work on callings shows that viewing work as meaningful correlates with higher satisfaction. Deci's meta-analysis demonstrates that external rewards can actually undermine intrinsic motivation, while Nye's research confirms interest-fit predicts both performance and persistence. O'Keefe offers a counterpoint—interests aren't fixed but can be developed over time. On compensation, Kahneman and Deaton found income improves life evaluation but not day-to-day emotional well-being beyond a threshold, though Killingsworth's recent work suggests wellbeing continues rising even at higher income levels. I should also include Humphrey's meta-analysis on work design factors like autonomy and significance.

I'm thinking I'll select around eight or nine of these studies to present a balanced view—acknowledging that interest matters for motivation while also showing that interests can be cultivated and that autonomy and leadership play crucial roles. This addresses both sides of the argument rather than treating it as one-dimensional.
answer:
**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012), "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research"** — Meta-analysis showing interest–job fit meaningfully predicts both performance and persistence, with stronger effects than commonly assumed. Supports your friends' claim that interest is underweighted relative to its actual impact.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, especially for complex, creative work like research. Extrinsic incentives mattered more for quantity-based, routine tasks.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Found a weak correlation (~.15) between pay level and job satisfaction across 92 studies. Suggests a modest salary bump is unlikely to drive lasting satisfaction differences.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)** — Cadets driven by internal motives had better long-term outcomes, and adding instrumental motives (status, money) actually weakened the benefits of internal motivation. A caution against taking a role primarily for external perks.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"** — Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus with income beyond a comfortable threshold. Note the counterpoint in **Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 Per Year"**, which found no plateau — so the money isn't worthless.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** — People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly, while interests can in fact be cultivated through engagement. A counterpoint to your friends: the "less interesting" role might become interesting, especially with the novelty of leading a team.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007), "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary"** — Job satisfaction is driven by multiple design features: autonomy, task significance, feedback, and social dimensions, not interest alone. Undermines the "focus almost exclusively on interestingness" framing, since leadership opportunities affect several of these levers.

**Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005), "The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect"** — Job satisfaction spikes after a voluntary move, then declines back toward baseline within a year or two. Worth factoring in when imagining how either choice will feel in 18 months.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right (interest is a stronger, more durable predictor of satisfaction and performance than pay), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — growth opportunities like leading a team are independently powerful, and interests are more malleable than they assume.
prompt:

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning:
I need to find well-known papers on intrinsic motivation, job satisfaction, and whether money matters less than we think. I'm being careful to only cite real studies I'm confident about, starting with Kahneman & Deaton's 2010 research showing that income boosts life satisfaction up to around $75,000 but doesn't significantly improve emotional well-being beyond that point.

Then there's Killingsworth's 2021 counterpoint suggesting income does continue to improve emotional well-being even above that threshold, followed by their 2023 collaborative resolution showing the relationship is more nuanced depending on baseline happiness levels. I'm also thinking about Amabile and Kramer's work on intrinsic motivation and the progress principle—how small wins and meaningful work drive engagement more than external rewards. Deci, Koestner & Ryan's meta-analysis is crucial here, demonstrating that extrinsic rewards can actually undermine intrinsic motivation. Wrzesniewski's research on jobs versus callings, and the West Point study showing that internal motives predict success while instrumental ones backfire, both support the idea that purpose matters more than pay. Judge's meta-analysis found only a weak correlation between salary and job satisfaction. I'm also considering Gilbert and Wilson's work on affective forecasting—how people systematically overestimate the emotional impact of life changes, including financial ones. And there's O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton's research on implicit theories of interest, which seems to be cut off but likely addresses whether passion is found or developed.

Now I'm looking at the remaining papers: Nikolova and Cnossen's work on what makes work meaningful, emphasizing autonomy, relatedness, and competence over pay; Levitt's coin-flip study showing people who make changes report higher happiness; and Cassar and Meier on nonmonetary incentives. The user asked for only paper names and two-sentence summaries, so I should organize these with supporting and complicating evidence, keeping each summary concise and grouped by theme.
answer:
**Supporting your friends' claim:**

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)** — A meta-analysis of 115 correlations found pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15. This suggests salary differences explain very little of how satisfied people are with their work.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)** — Tracking over 10,000 cadets across 14 years, internal motives (genuine interest) predicted long-term success, while instrumental motives (career advancement, money) actually *reduced* outcomes even when combined with internal ones. Adding extrinsic reasons to intrinsic interest appeared to dilute motivation rather than boost it.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation" (Psychological Bulletin)** — Across 128 experiments, tangible rewards tended to undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. This supports the idea that chasing external incentives can erode the enjoyment that sustains long-term engagement.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)** — Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work. Day-to-day engagement with the work itself outweighed recognition, incentives, and other factors.

**Complicating the claim:**

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved" (PNAS)** — This adversarial collaboration found that happiness does continue rising with income for most people, contradicting the famous "$75k plateau" finding. Money matters more than the popular interpretation of earlier research suggested.

**Herzberg, Mausner & Snyderman (1959), "The Motivation to Work"** — The classic two-factor theory holds that intrinsic factors (interesting work, achievement) drive satisfaction, but also that *responsibility and advancement* — like leading a team — are among the strongest motivators. Your leadership opportunity falls squarely in the "motivator" category, not just the "hygiene" one.

**Nikolova & Cnossen (2020), "What Makes Work Meaningful and Why Economists Should Care About It" (Labour Economics)** — Using European survey data, autonomy, competence, and relatedness explained far more of work meaningfulness than income or benefits. Notably, this cuts both ways for you: a leadership role can increase autonomy and competence-development even if the subject matter is less interesting.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)** — People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly, while those with a "growth" theory of interest develop passion for initially less-interesting areas. The OpenAI work being "much less interesting" today may not be a fixed property.

**Short answer:** Your friends are directionally right — the literature consistently shows intrinsic interest beats pay — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, especially since team leadership is itself a well-documented intrinsic motivator, not just an extrinsic perk.

answer:
**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Foundational work showing that well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness*, with intrinsic interest being a major but not sole driver. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and competence needs, so "interesting" isn't the only path to fulfillment.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" (PNAS)** — Cadets with strong internal motives had better long-term career outcomes, while adding instrumental motives (status, money) actually weakened this effect. Supports your friends' view that prioritizing intrinsic interest over external perks pays off.

**Judge et al. (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Across 92 samples, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15. A "bit higher salary" is unlikely to meaningfully move your happiness.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** — Intrinsic motivation is a medium-to-strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic factors still matter, especially for quantity-based outcomes. Suggests interest matters a lot, but "almost exclusively" overstates the evidence.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** — People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly; those with a "growth" theory of interest develop passion for initially unexciting work. Relevant caveat: the OpenAI role might become interesting once you're leading and shaping the work.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting"** — People systematically mispredict how future choices will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like "less interesting") and underweighting adaptation. Whatever feels decisive right now may matter less than you think within a year.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (HBR, based on *The Progress Principle*)** — Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress in *meaningful* work. Meaning can come from interesting problems — or from building and growing a team you care about.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right (intrinsic interest beats marginal salary by a wide margin), but "almost exclusively" isn't supported — autonomy, growth, and the chance to lead are themselves strong intrinsic motivators, and interest in a role is more malleable than it feels today.

answer:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature"** (Journal of Vocational Behavior)
A meta-analysis of 86 studies found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). This suggests salary differences, especially modest ones, predict little about how happy you'll be at work.

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012) — "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research"** (Perspectives on Psychological Science)
This meta-analysis found that fit between a person's interests and their work predicts both performance and persistence better than previously believed. Interest-job fit was a stronger predictor than many traditional selection criteria.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) — "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"** (PNAS)
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus well below typical Meta/OpenAI compensation levels, while income mainly boosts abstract life evaluation. A "bit higher salary" at your income level likely buys very little additional happiness.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005) — "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"** (Current Directions in Psychological Science)
People systematically overestimate how much external changes (raises, titles) will affect their happiness — the "impact bias." Daily experienced quality of work tends to matter more than people predict.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins"** (Harvard Business Review, based on diary studies of 12,000 work days)
The single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress in *meaningful* work. Uninteresting work makes this progress loop much harder to sustain.

**Papers complicating the "almost exclusively" part:**

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** (Psychological Science)
Interest is not fixed — people with a "growth theory" of interest develop passion for new domains, while "fit theorists" give up when work gets hard. The OpenAI role could become interesting; the Meta role could stop being so.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** (American Psychologist)
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interest alone. Leading a team (something you say you've always wanted) speaks directly to needs this framework says you shouldn't ignore.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) — "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"** (Academy of Management Review)
People actively reshape the tasks and boundaries of their jobs to make them more meaningful, especially in roles with managerial discretion. A team-lead position may give you *more* power to make the work interesting over time, not less.

**Bottom line per the literature:** your friends are directionally right that interestingness is underrated relative to pay — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since long-desired growth opportunities (like leading a team) tap the same intrinsic-motivation system the research celebrates.

answer:
**Papers supporting your friends' view:**

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Argues that autonomy, competence, and intrinsic interest are core psychological needs, and that work satisfying these needs predicts well-being far better than external rewards. Extrinsic incentives can even undermine intrinsic motivation in some conditions.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Finds the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). Suggests marginal salary increases buy much less satisfaction than people expect.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (HBR; based on *The Progress Principle*)** — Diary studies of knowledge workers found that making progress on meaningful, engaging work was the single strongest driver of positive inner work life. Day-to-day engagement with the work itself mattered more than recognition or incentives.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work (like research). However, it also shows extrinsic incentives matter independently — so "almost exclusively" overstates it.

**Papers complicating your friends' view:**

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"** — Shows people can reshape tasks and relationships to make less interesting roles more meaningful. Implies a "boring" leadership role isn't fixed — you may be able to craft interest into it.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** — People who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets hard; interests can be developed, not just found. Treating "interestingness" as a static property of a job is empirically shaky.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis"** — Person-job and person-organization fit (values, goals, growth needs) predict satisfaction and retention. If leading a team is a long-held growth goal, that's a fit dimension distinct from task interest.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting"** — People systematically mispredict how future choices will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like "interesting now") and underestimating adaptation. Relevant to both directions of your decision.

**Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones (2012), "What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?"** — People knowingly choose options that don't maximize moment-to-moment happiness, in favor of purpose, status, and long-term goals. Suggests happiness from interesting work isn't the only legitimate criterion.

**Bottom line:** The research supports "interestingness is undervalued relative to salary," but not "focus on it almost exclusively" — your long-held goal of leading a team is exactly the kind of fit/growth factor the literature says also predicts satisfaction.

**"Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" — Ryan & Deci (2000, American Psychologist)**
Foundational work showing that well-being and sustained motivation depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, a leadership role can serve these needs (especially competence and autonomy) even when task content is less stimulating.

**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010, Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Across 92 samples, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction, meaning salary differences explain very little of how happy people are at work. This is the strongest evidence in your friends' favor: a "bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

**"Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014, Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, while incentives predict quantity, and both operate together rather than one dominating. This supports valuing interest highly — but contradicts the "almost exclusively" framing.

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997, Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who view their work as a calling report substantially higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Caveat: calling orientation was found across occupations, suggesting it's partly about your relationship to the work, not just the work's objective interestingness.

**"Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" — Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001, Academy of Management Review)**
Employees actively reshape task boundaries and meaning to make jobs more engaging, meaning a "less interesting" job is not a fixed quantity. A team-lead role typically grants *more* latitude for this kind of crafting.

**"Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" — O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018, Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly from new domains, while those who believe interests develop sustain engagement and find new areas compelling over time. This cuts against treating current interestingness as a permanent property of the OpenAI role.

**"The Power of Small Wins" — Amabile & Kramer (2011, Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that making progress in meaningful work was the single biggest driver of positive inner work life, beating recognition and incentives. Relevant nuance: "meaningful" was driven by perceived progress and impact, not by intellectual fascination per se.

**"Affective Forecasting" — Wilson & Gilbert (2003, Advances in Experimental Social Psychology)**
People systematically overestimate both the intensity and duration of emotional reactions to future events, including job changes. Whatever you predict you'll feel about losing interesting work — or gaining a team — is likely exaggerated in both directions.

**Net read of the literature:** your friends are half right — salary is genuinely overrated, and interest/intrinsic motivation is genuinely powerful — but "almost exclusively" is unsupported, because autonomy, growth, and a long-held aspiration (leading a team) are exactly the kind of need-satisfying factors the same research says matter most.

People who believe interests are fixed give up quickly when new domains get difficult, while those who see interests as developable cultivate passion in initially "boring" areas. This directly challenges treating current interestingness as a fixed property of the OpenAI role.

**"Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"** (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001, *Academy of Management Review*)
Employees actively reshape task boundaries and meaning in their roles, meaning "how interesting a job is" is partly under your control, especially with the autonomy of a team lead. A less interesting role today is not a fixed sentence.

**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** (Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*)
Pay level correlates only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction, so the "bit higher salary" should probably carry little weight in your decision. This partially supports your friends: compensation is genuinely overrated.

**"Affective Forecasting"** (Wilson & Gilbert, 2003, *Advances in Experimental Social Psychology*)
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of their emotional reactions to future events, including career changes. Both your prediction that the new work will feel boring and that team leadership will feel fulfilling are likely exaggerated.

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** (Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997, *Journal of Research in Personality*)
People who view work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. However, calling orientation was found across all occupations studied, suggesting it stems from the person's relationship to work more than from the work itself.

**"The Power of Small Wins"** (Amabile & Kramer, 2011, *Harvard Business Review*)
Day-to-day progress on meaningful work was the single biggest driver of positive inner work life in a study of ~12,000 diary entries. Worth noting: "meaningful" here includes work that matters to you or your team — such as a leadership goal you've always wanted — not just intellectually interesting work.

**Bottom line implied by the literature:** Your friends are half right — interest and intrinsic motivation are weighted heavily and salary is weighted too heavily — but "almost exclusively" is not supported. Interest is malleable, autonomy and growth (which leadership offers) are equally core motivators, and your forecast of how each job will feel is probably unreliable.
prompt:

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I'm thinking through what research actually shows about whether interesting work should be the primary focus. The evidence is more nuanced than that — while intrinsic motivation and meaningful work do matter significantly, they're not the only factors that determine job satisfaction and wellbeing. I should pull together some real papers on motivation, job satisfaction, and meaning at work to give an accurate picture of what the research actually supports. I'm narrowing down to the strongest 6-8 papers and deciding on framing. The key ones are Self-Determination Theory, The Progress Principle, job satisfaction research, person-job fit, and the job characteristics model—these directly address how interest, autonomy, and meaningful work drive satisfaction. I'll keep the summaries concise and let them show that while interest matters significantly, it's not the only factor; fit, growth opportunity, and work design all play important roles. A brief closing line acknowledging this balance should wrap it up cleanly.
answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (*American Psychologist*)**
Argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — intrinsic interest is central but not sufficient alone. Notably, leading a team can also serve autonomy and competence needs, so the framework doesn't cleanly favor "interesting" over "leadership."

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (*Harvard Business Review*; based on *The Progress Principle*)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers found that the single biggest driver of positive "inner work life" is making progress in meaningful work. Supports your friends' view, with the caveat that "meaningful" includes feeling effective and unblocked, not just intellectually interesting.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (*Journal of Research in Personality*)**
People who view their work as a "calling" report higher life and job satisfaction than those who view it as a job (money) or career (advancement). However, the same work can be experienced as any of the three — orientation matters as much as the job's content.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" (*PNAS*)**
Found that day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75K (then-dollars), suggesting marginal salary gains at senior tech compensation levels buy little happiness. Note that Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023, *PNAS*) revised this in an adversarial collaboration — happiness does keep rising with income for most people, though the effect is modest.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting" (*Advances in Experimental Social Psychology*)**
Documents the "impact bias": people systematically overestimate how strongly and how long future events (like a new title or raise) will affect their happiness. This cuts both ways — you may also overestimate how miserable less-interesting work will make you.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work" (*Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*)**
The Job Characteristics Model identifies five drivers of motivation: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. Interesting work maps mainly onto variety and significance, but a leadership role can score higher on autonomy and identity.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work" (*Personnel Psychology*)**
Meta-analysis showing person–job and person–organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, and retention. Implies the right question isn't "interesting vs. leadership" in the abstract, but which role fits *your* values and strengths.

**Benson, Li & Shue (2019), "Promotions and the Peter Principle" (*Quarterly Journal of Economics*)**
Using sales data, shows firms promote strong individual contributors into management even when this predicts poor managerial performance, harming both the firm and the individual. Worth considering whether "leading a small team" is something you'd actually be good at and enjoy, versus something that sounds appealing.

**Bottom line:** the research strongly supports that interest/meaning matters more than people expect and salary deltas matter less — but "almost exclusively" overstates it; autonomy, fit, progress, and growth (which a leadership role might provide) are comparably important.

answer:
**Supporting your friends' claim:**

**"Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" — Ryan & Deci (2000)**
Intrinsic motivation, driven by autonomy, competence, and relatedness, is a strong predictor of well-being and sustained performance. Extrinsic rewards like salary do far less to sustain engagement over time.

Across 86 studies, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15. People at very different salary levels report remarkably similar satisfaction with their jobs.

People who view their work as intrinsically meaningful (a "calling") report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. This held even within the same occupation.

Tangible rewards can actually undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Trading interest for money may erode the engagement you currently have.

**Complicating your friends' claim:**

People who believe interests are fixed give up on new areas quickly, while those with a "growth" theory of interest develop passion for initially unexciting domains. The OpenAI role could become interesting, especially since leading a team is something you've "always wanted."

**"Affective Forecasting" — Wilson & Gilbert (2003)**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overestimating both the intensity and duration of emotional reactions. Your prediction that less-interesting work will make you unhappy is itself unreliable.

**"The Temporal Pattern to the Experience of Regret" — Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**
In the long run, people regret inactions (chances not taken) far more than actions. Declining the leadership opportunity you've always wanted may be the choice that stings later.

reasoning: I'm thinking through which papers would be most relevant here—I should draw on established research about job satisfaction and what actually drives it. answer:
**Kahneman, D., Krueger, A. B., Schkade, D., Schwarz, N., & Stone, A. A. (2006). "Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion." *Science*.**
Shows that people drastically overestimate how much income improvements affect day-to-day happiness because the salary difference is salient at decision time but fades from attention afterward. Supports the idea that the pay bump will matter less than it feels like it will right now.

**Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010). "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis." *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.**

**Wrzesniewski, A., McCauley, C., Rozin, P., & Schwartz, B. (1997). "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work." *Journal of Research in Personality*.**
People who view work as intrinsically meaningful ("callings") report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Partial support for your friends' claim.

**Ryan, R. M., & Deci, E. L. (2000). "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being." *American Psychologist*.**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team you've always wanted to lead could satisfy autonomy and competence needs, complicating a pure "interestingness" rule.

**Cerasoli, C. P., Nicklin, J. M., & Ford, M. T. (2014). "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis." *Psychological Bulletin*.**
Intrinsic motivation is the stronger predictor of work quality, but extrinsic incentives still independently predict performance, especially for quantity-based outcomes. Evidence against the "almost exclusively" framing — both channels matter.

**Amabile, T. M., & Kramer, S. J. (2011). *The Progress Principle* (and the underlying diary study, "The Power of Small Wins," *HBR*).**
Analysis of ~12,000 daily work diaries found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on *meaningful* work, more than recognition or incentives. Meaning and progress, not raw "interestingness," are the active ingredients — a role with more ownership can score highly here.

**Wilson, T. D., & Gilbert, D. T. (2005). "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want." *Current Directions in Psychological Science*.**
People are systematically bad at predicting how future circumstances will make them feel, both overestimating the impact of single salient factors and underestimating adaptation. A caution against confidently forecasting that *either* the interesting work or the team-lead role will deliver as imagined.

**Hsee, C. K., & Hastie, R. (2006). "Decision and Experience: Why Don't We Choose What Makes Us Happy?" *Trends in Cognitive Sciences*.**
People predictably choose options that score well on quantifiable, justifiable dimensions (salary, title) over options that score well on experiential ones (daily engagement). **Duffy, R. D., & Dik, B. J. (2013). "Research on Calling: What Have We Learned and Where Are We Going?" *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.**

**Net of the literature:** your friends are directionally right — experienced engagement is underweighted relative to salary — but "almost exclusively" overstates it; the unmet desire to lead maps onto autonomy/growth needs that the same literature says matter a lot.

People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overweighting salient differences (like "less interesting work") while underestimating adaptation. Cuts both ways: you'd likely adapt to both the boredom and the management excitement faster than you expect.

Whatever feature you're currently focusing on (here: interestingness) looms larger in your decision than it will in your daily experience. Your friends' framing may itself be inducing a focusing illusion.

**Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones (2012), "What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?" (American Economic Review)**

**Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** (Hackman & Oldham, 1976, *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*)

Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves life evaluation modestly but day-to-day emotional well-being little (Killingsworth's later data show some continued effect, but small). **Bottom line implied by the literature:** your friends are directionally right that interest and intrinsic motivation beat pay, but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, and meaning (which leading a team may provide) are equally well-supported predictors, and interest itself can develop in a new role.

reasoning:

answer:
**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**
Across 115 studies, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15), suggesting salary differences predict happiness at work far less than people assume. This is the strongest evidence for your friends' position.

**"Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" — Ryan & Deci (2000)**
Motivation and well-being depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not just task interest. Note that leading a team you wanted to lead may satisfy autonomy and competence needs even if the content is less interesting.

**"Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**
The Job Characteristics Model shows that skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback jointly drive internal motivation — "interestingness" is only one ingredient. A leadership role can score higher on autonomy and significance while lower on intrinsic task interest.

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**
People who see work as a calling report substantially higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward money or advancement. However, orientation is partly about how you relate to work, not just the work's objective content.

**"Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction" — Schkade & Kahneman (1998)**
People overweight whatever feature they're currently focusing on when predicting future happiness. This cuts both ways for you: you may be overweighting "interestingness" while deciding, or overweighting salary and title.

**"Affective Forecasting" — Wilson & Gilbert (2003)**
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of their emotional reactions to future events (the "impact bias"). The excitement of a new title and the pain of boring work will both likely fade faster than you expect.

**"What Makes Work Meaningful and Why Economists Should Care About It" — Nikolova & Cnossen (2020)**
Using European survey data, autonomy, relatedness, and competence explain far more of work meaningfulness than income or benefits do. Again, meaningfulness ≠ interestingness; managing people can be a strong source of relatedness.

**"The Power of Small Wins" — Amabile & Kramer (2011, based on *The Progress Principle* diary studies)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that making progress in meaningful work is the single biggest driver of positive inner work life. A role where you're set up to make visible progress (even on less fascinating problems) may beat a fascinating role where you're stuck.

**"Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis" — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**
Person-job and person-organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, and retention. The right question isn't "interesting vs. salary" in the abstract, but which role fits your values and the identity you want (e.g., researcher vs. leader).

**Bottom line implied by the literature:** your friends are half right — pay is genuinely overrated — but the research supports intrinsic motivation broadly (autonomy, growth, meaning, progress), not task interestingness "almost exclusively." A leadership role you've always wanted is itself an intrinsic motivator, so this isn't actually an interest-vs.-money tradeoff.

Foundational self-determination theory paper showing that intrinsic motivation and satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs predict well-being better than external rewards. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and competence needs — so "interestingness" isn't the only path to intrinsic fulfillment.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014). "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis." *Psychological Bulletin.***
Across 183 studies, intrinsic motivation was a strong predictor of performance, especially performance *quality*, while incentives better predicted quantity. Supports your friends' direction, but shows the two *jointly* predict outcomes — not that interest matters "almost exclusively."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010). "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis." *Journal of Vocational Behavior.***
Pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only about r = .15, and people at very different salary levels report similar satisfaction. Suggests a "bit higher salary" should carry very little weight in your decision.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997). "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work." *Journal of Research in Personality.***
People who view their work as a calling report significantly higher life and work satisfaction than those with job (money) or career (advancement) orientations. The catch: a calling orientation can come from meaning and impact, not only from intellectual interestingness.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976). "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory." *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance.***
The job characteristics model shows motivation comes from five factors: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not interestingness alone. A team-lead role can score high on several of these even if the topic is less fascinating.

**Benson, Li & Shue (2019). "Promotions and the Peter Principle." *Quarterly Journal of Economics.***
Firms promote their best individual contributors into management, where many underperform because IC skill doesn't predict management skill. A useful caution to test whether you actually *enjoy* leading, not just the idea of it — though you've wanted it for a while, which is a meaningful signal.

**Schkade & Kahneman (1998). "Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction." *Psychological Science.***
When comparing options, people massively overweight whatever dimension is salient at decision time and underweight everything else. Whichever factor you (or your friends) are currently fixated on — interestingness or leadership — is likely being overweighted right now.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003). "Affective Forecasting." *Advances in Experimental Social Psychology.***
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of their future emotions, adapting faster than expected to both good and bad changes. Both the excitement of leading a team and the dullness of less interesting work will likely fade faster than you predict.

Bottom line from the literature: your friends are directionally right (interest and intrinsic motivation are strong predictors; salary is weak), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, and long-held aspirations like leadership are independently powerful predictors of satisfaction.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in Meta doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from OpenAI - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning:
I need to find legitimate research papers on intrinsic motivation and interest rather than making up citations. I'm thinking about Deci and Ryan's self-determination theory, Amabile's work on intrinsic motivation, and Wrzesniewski's research comparing intrinsic versus extrinsic motivations—these are well-established frameworks I can draw from to answer whether focusing on interesting work is actually supported by research. I'm adding more papers that support intrinsic motivation's importance—Deci and Ryan's self-determination theory, Cerasoli's meta-analysis showing both intrinsic and extrinsic factors matter (which actually argues against the "almost exclusively" framing), and Kahneman & Deaton's finding that income plateaus around $75k. I'm also including O'Keefe on how interests can be developed rather than just discovered, which is relevant to whether the OpenAI role could become engaging over time. The affective forecasting work from Wilson & Gilbert is worth noting since people often misjudge how they'll feel about future situations.

I'm considering whether to add Gilovich & Medvec on regret—people tend to regret inactions more than actions—which could frame the decision differently. I'm aiming for 7-8 papers total and keeping the format clean with just the citation and a brief summary, though I'm debating whether to add a short closing line that frames the verdict or stick purely to the papers as requested.
answer:
**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) — "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"** (*Psychological Bulletin*)
A meta-analysis of 128 experiments showing that tangible rewards tend to undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Supports your friends' view that intrinsic interest is a fragile and valuable resource not easily substituted by pay.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014) — "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets"** (*PNAS*)
Tracking ~10,000 cadets over 14 years, internal motives predicted long-term success, while holding strong instrumental motives (money, status) alongside internal ones actually *worsened* outcomes. Suggests optimizing for external rewards can dilute the benefit of doing something you find meaningful.

**Judge et al. (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** (*Journal of Vocational Behavior*)
Across 92 samples, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15. A "bit higher salary" is a weak predictor of how happy you'll be in the role.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** (*Psychological Bulletin*)
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality-focused work, but extrinsic incentives matter too and the two coexist. This is a partial counterpoint to "focus almost exclusively" — it's "both," with intrinsic motivation weighted heavily for complex work.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) — "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"** (*Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*)
The job characteristics model shows satisfaction and motivation flow from skill variety, autonomy, task significance, and feedback — not just task interestingness. Relevant because leading a team can score high on autonomy and significance even if the subject matter is duller.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** (*Psychological Science*)
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests are developed sustain engagement in new domains. A complication for your friends' claim: the "less interesting" OpenAI work might become interesting, especially combined with a long-desired leadership role.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) — "Affective Forecasting"** (*Advances in Experimental Social Psychology*)
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overweighting salient features of a choice (like topic interestingness or salary) and underweighting adaptation. A caution against confident predictions in either direction.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** (*American Psychologist*)
Well-being at work depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — interest is one route to these, not the only one. A leadership role you've "always wanted" may serve autonomy and competence needs that pure task-interest doesn't.

---

Net read of the literature: your friends are directionally right (interest/intrinsic motivation is empirically underrated relative to salary), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — the leadership and growth dimension you mentioned is itself a well-documented driver of intrinsic motivation.

answer:

A meta-analysis of 86 studies found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). This is the strongest evidence behind your friends' claim — salary differences predict satisfaction far less than people assume.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** (American Psychologist)

**Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005), "The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect"** (Journal of Applied Psychology)
Job changers experience a satisfaction spike that fades significantly within a year. Relevant caution: the excitement of a new role and title at OpenAI would likely fade, leaving you with the day-to-day reality of less interesting work.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis"** (Personnel Psychology)

Contradicting the famous Kahneman & Deaton plateau finding, this large experience-sampling study found well-being continues rising with income with no plateau. A counterpoint to "money doesn't matter" — though the effect of a modest salary bump is small in absolute terms.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"** (Current Directions in Psychological Science)
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overweighting salient factors at decision time. Whatever feels most vivid right now (the team-lead title, or the fear of boring work) is probably being overweighted in your deliberation.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right that intrinsic interest is underrated relative to pay, but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, fit with long-held goals (like leading), and meaning all carry comparable weight, and your desire to manage is itself an intrinsic motivator the research takes seriously.

A meta-analysis of 86 studies found the correlation between salary and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). People earning very different amounts report nearly identical satisfaction levels, suggesting pay differences like yours matter less than expected.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who view their work as a "calling" (intrinsically meaningful) report significantly higher work and life satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations. This held even within the same occupation.

This meta-analysis of 128 experiments found that contingent extrinsic rewards can actually undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Chasing external incentives at the cost of interest can erode the engagement that made work enjoyable.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) – "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review) / *The Progress Principle***
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work. Boring work makes daily progress feel hollow, dragging down motivation and creativity.

**Complicating the "almost exclusively" part:**

**Ryan & Deci (2000) – "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Well-being depends on three needs—autonomy, competence, and *relatedness*—not interest alone. A leadership role granting more autonomy and growth could satisfy these needs even with less interesting subject matter.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005) – "Consequences of individuals' fit at work: A meta-analysis" (Personnel Psychology)**
Job satisfaction is predicted by multiple types of fit—person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor—not just task content. A role that fits long-held goals (like leading a team) is itself a strong satisfaction predictor.

**Sherman et al. (2012) – "Leadership is associated with lower levels of stress" (PNAS)**
Leaders showed lower cortisol and anxiety than non-leaders, largely explained by an increased sense of control. If leading a team is a genuine long-term aspiration, the role itself may carry well-being benefits beyond task interest.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) – "Affective Forecasting" (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology)**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overestimating both the intensity and duration of emotional reactions. You may be overestimating both how much you'd miss the interesting work and how much you'd enjoy leading a team.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) – "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" (Academy of Management Review)**
Employees can actively reshape task and relational boundaries of their roles to increase meaning and interest. "How interesting a job is" is partly malleable—especially with the influence that comes from leading a team.

**Bottom line from the literature:** Your friends are directionally right—intrinsic interest is a stronger predictor of satisfaction than pay—but "almost exclusively" overstates it; autonomy, fit with long-term goals, and a sense of control (all of which the leadership role may offer) are comparably powerful predictors.

answer:
**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) — "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**
This meta-analysis of 128 studies found that tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. It supports the idea that chasing pay over interest carries real motivational costs, though effects vary by context.

Across 92 samples, pay level correlated only about r = .15 with job satisfaction. A modestly higher salary is a weak predictor of how happy you'll be in a role.

People who experience their work as a calling report substantially higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. However, "calling" includes meaning and contribution, not just task-level interestingness.

People who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets hard, while those who believe interest can be developed sustain engagement in new domains. This cuts against your friends' claim: the OpenAI role could become interesting as you grow into it.

People systematically mispredict how future changes will make them feel, overweighting salient differences (salary, title) and underweighting daily experience. Your day-to-day tasks will likely dominate your wellbeing more than either the raise or the title.

One honest caveat: the literature supports "interest and intrinsic motivation are underrated relative to pay," but no research supports focusing on it *almost exclusively* — autonomy, growth, and the fact that interest is malleable all matter too.

prompt:

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I need to find actual research papers on intrinsic motivation, job satisfaction, and interest that speak to whether interesting work should be the primary focus. The research picture is more nuanced than my friends suggest — while intrinsic interest clearly matters a lot for engagement and satisfaction, it's not the whole story, and I should cite real studies that show both the importance of interesting work and its limitations. Kahneman and Deaton's work on income plateaus, plus Cerasoli's meta-analysis showing both intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives matter for performance, suggest the friends' "almost exclusively" framing is too narrow. I should also note the focusing illusion—people tend to overweight salient factors when predicting happiness. I'll format this as paper names with two-sentence summaries and close with a brief verdict that acknowledges management roles do offer autonomy and growth, but research shows multiple factors influence satisfaction and performance.
answer:
**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Deci & Ryan (2000)
Argues that well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, a leadership role can serve autonomy and competence even if the task content is less fascinating.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)
Found intrinsic motivation is a medium-to-strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, while incentives predict quantity. Supports your friends partially: interest matters a lot, but it works *jointly* with extrinsic factors rather than exclusively.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012)
Meta-analysis showing interest–job fit meaningfully predicts performance and persistence, more than earlier research assumed. This is probably the strongest evidence for your friends' "interest is underrated" claim.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)
Found pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). Suggests the "bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)
People who see work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward money or advancement. The relevant question is which job you'd experience as a calling — interesting research, or finally leading a team.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006)
Shows people exaggerate the importance of any single factor when their attention is focused on it. A caution in both directions: you may currently be overweighting either "interestingness" or "team lead" because it's salient.

**The Progress Principle** — Amabile & Kramer (2011)
Diary studies of knowledge workers found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work day to day. Meaningfulness and visible progress mattered more than how intellectually exciting the domain was.

**Bottom line:** the research supports "interest is underrated relative to pay," but not "focus on it almost exclusively" — autonomy, growth (e.g., a long-desired leadership role), and progress are comparably strong predictors.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in Meta doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from OpenAI - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning:
I need to find legitimate research papers on intrinsic motivation and interest to answer whether the claim is well-supported. I'm thinking through papers from Self-Determination Theory and work on intrinsic motivation, but I also need to present a balanced view since research shows other factors matter too—things like competence, autonomy, and external rewards can interact with interest in complex ways.

I'm compiling a list of well-known papers: Ryan and Deci's foundational work on self-determination, Amabile's research on progress and meaningful work, Wrzesniewski's study on how people view their work as callings versus jobs, and meta-analyses showing that pay has a weaker relationship with satisfaction than people expect. I'm also noting affective forecasting research that shows people often misjudge what will make them happy, sometimes overestimating the impact of single factors like interesting work while underestimating others.

I'm considering whether to include papers on how interests develop rather than just being discovered, and whether leadership aspirations themselves might be worth citing—though I'm still deciding if some of these are tangential. The goal is to present a balanced view that acknowledges multiple factors beyond just the appeal of the role itself, formatted as paper names with brief summaries as requested.
answer:
**Papers supporting your friends' claim:**

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Foundational work showing that intrinsic motivation—driven by autonomy, competence, and relatedness—is a core driver of sustained well-being and engagement. Extrinsic rewards like salary can even undermine intrinsic motivation under some conditions.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 86 studies, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15—remarkably weak. This suggests a modest salary bump alone is a poor reason to switch jobs.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who see their work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations. Notably, the "career" orientation—chasing promotions and leadership—didn't predict the same satisfaction gains.

**Papers complicating the claim:**

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation matters a lot, but extrinsic incentives independently predict performance too—they're not mutually exclusive. The "exclusively" part of your friends' claim isn't well supported.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
People who believe interests are fixed give up on new areas quickly; those who believe interests are developed find engagement in initially "boring" work. Relevant because the leadership role you've "always wanted" could become interesting once you're in it.

**Wilson, Wheatley, Meyers, Gilbert & Axsom (2000), "Focalism: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting"**
People drastically overweight whatever single factor they're focusing on when predicting future happiness—exactly what happens when you fixate on "interestingness" (or salary, or leadership) in isolation. The factor you're currently obsessing over will likely matter less than you think.

**Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones (2012), "What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?"**
People's actual choices systematically deviate from pure happiness-maximization, weighing purpose, status, and long-term goals. Your stated long-term desire to lead a team is a legitimate input, not noise to ignore.

**Benson, Li & Shue (2019), "Promotions and the Peter Principle"**
Strong individual contributors are often promoted into management despite that role requiring different skills, sometimes reducing both performance and satisfaction. A useful caution: test whether you actually want to *manage*, or just like the idea of having wanted it.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in Meta doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from OpenAI - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning: I'm thinking through which papers would actually address the user's question about whether interesting work is underrated compared to salary, leadership, and growth opportunities. I need to cite real, well-known research on intrinsic motivation, job satisfaction, and meaningful work, while being careful not to invent papers. I should also acknowledge the research showing people often mispredicts what makes them happy, and include critiques of the "follow your passion" narrative. I'm finishing up the citation for that paper on implicit theories and passion, then noting some key counterpoints around leadership and autonomy. I want to make sure I'm presenting a balanced view—showing that while intrinsic motivation and fit matter, pay and management roles still play a role through autonomy and competence. I'm planning to stick to the user's request for just paper names and two-sentence summaries, keeping it to around 7-8 papers total.
answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) – "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
A meta-analysis of 92 studies found pay level correlates only ~0.15 with job satisfaction, meaning salary explains very little of how happy people are with their jobs. This supports the claim that pay differences (especially modest ones) are commonly overweighted in career decisions.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) – "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus at moderate income levels, while only abstract life evaluation keeps rising with income. A small raise on top of an already-high tech salary is unlikely to change how you actually feel day to day.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who see their work as a "calling" (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward pay ("job") or advancement ("career"). This is the strongest evidence for your friends' position—orientation toward the work itself predicts satisfaction better than rewards or promotion.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) – "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, and it predicts performance even when incentives are present. However, extrinsic incentives also matter independently—so the evidence supports "interest matters a lot," not "interest matters almost exclusively."

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) – "The Power of Small Wins" (and *The Progress Principle*)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Notably, "meaningful" here includes work you find significant—but progress and good day-to-day conditions mattered as much as the topic itself.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) – "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests can be developed sustain motivation across domains. This cuts against your friends: the OpenAI role might become interesting as you grow into it, especially the new challenge of leading a team.

**Chen, Ellsworth & Schwarz (2015) – "Finding a Fit or Developing It: Implicit Theories About Achieving Passion for Work"**
"Fit theorists" prioritize current enjoyment when choosing jobs but don't end up happier long-term than "develop theorists," who expect passion to grow with investment. Choosing purely on current interestingness is not clearly the winning strategy the "research shows."

**Ryan & Deci (2000) – "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not interestingness alone. A leadership role you've always wanted may serve autonomy and competence needs in ways that staying as an IC, even on fascinating problems, does not.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005) – "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting a single salient dimension (here, "interestingness" or "team lead title"). Whichever factor dominates your imagination right now is probably exerting more pull than it will exert on your actual future happiness.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are roughly half right—interest/intrinsic motivation is genuinely underrated relative to salary—but the evidence does not support weighting it "almost exclusively," especially against a long-held goal (leadership) that maps onto core psychological needs.
